Authors: Clara Talca Onken; Andrea Galli; Andrea Giuliani; Valdecir A. Janasi; Quentin Charbonnier; Petrus le Roux; Philip E. Janney; Max W. Schmidt

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123469

Relevance: 0.587 (consider)

Most post-orogenic magmatic suites are dominated by granitoid rocks and “crustal” isotopic signatures, commonly interpreted as intra-crustal melting or interaction of mantle-derived melts with crustal materials. This interpretation is challenged in the Cambrian Araçuaí belt (SE Brazil), whose post-orogenic suite has a northern part similarly dominated by granites and charnockites, but a southern part exposing lower crustal composite plutons with gabbros, diorites, occasional syenites and minor granites. This study characterizes one of these plutons, Mimoso do Sul (dated here to 494–501 Ma), which exposes (i) mafic rocks that range from MORB-like tholeiitic norites to enriched potassic monzogabbros and (ii) a highly alkaline, potassic suite, including glimmerites, monzogabbros, monzodiorites, syenites and granites. The most primitive gabbros (X Mg = 0.75–0.60) show age-corrected 87 Sr/ 86 Sr and εNd values of ~0.7078 and − 10.5, different from any regional country rocks - ruling out a derivation by crustal contamination. Instead, the arc-like trace element signature, including negative HFSE anomalies and elevated LILE, was likely imparted by subduction-related fluids or melts infiltrating the lithospheric mantle during the formation of the Rio Doce Arc (~600 Ma). Yet, the highly unradiogenic Nd is at odds with typical subduction-zone signatures and requires an additional ancient metasomatim of the lithosphere. The diverse mafic rock types have indistinguishable isotopic compositions, but quite distinct trace element characteristics, interpreted to reflect variably intense metasomatism in the sub-orogenic mantle lithosphere and varying degrees of melting thereof. Further, for the alkaline differentiation suite, major and trace elements and Sr Nd isotopes support a nearly closed-system fractionation with some (unsystematic) Sr isotope variability due to minor crustal assimilation. Extended to post-orogenic magmatism in general, our results imply that the “crustal” signature and the variable magmatic lineages arise from a heterogeneously metasomatized sub-orogenic lithospheric mantle (SOLM).

item.title: Multi-generation mineral growth and phase equilibrium modelling: Tracing the long history of granitoids in continental collisional orogen
item.dc:creator: Xin-Yue Guo
Qiong-Xia Xia
Er-Lin Zhu
Zi-Fu Zhao
Ren-Xu Chen
item.description: Abstract An integrated petrographic, mineralogic, geochronologic and phase equilibrium modelling study of Jingshan granites reveals four distinct metamorphic-anatectic-magmatic-hydrothermal events recorded in zircon and garnet. Zircon geochronology identifies Paleoproterozoic, Neoproterozoic and Triassic (240–200 Ma) ages in inherited domains, alongside Late Jurassic (~160 Ma) ages in anatectic/magmatic domains. These data indicate that the Jingshan granites originated from remelting of crustal materials that experienced Triassic metamorphism. Four generations of garnet are distinguished. Metamorphic garnet (Grt I), formed during Triassic metamorphism, exhibits flat to steep middle to heavy rare earth element (M-HREE) patterns. Peritectic garnet (Grt II), produced during Late Jurassic crustal anatexis, displays Y-HREE-enrichment and high (Yb/Dy)N ratios. Magmatic garnet (Grt III), crystallized during magma cooling, exhibits negative Eu anomalies and flat to steep M-HREE distributions. Hydrothermal garnet (Grt IV), formed during the late magmatic-hydrothermal stage, shows low TiO2 contents and positive Eu anomalies. Phase equilibrium modelling constrains partial melting conditions to 720–755°C and 1.2–1.3 GPa (corresponding to depths of ~ 38–44 km in the lower crust), occurring via the fluid-present melting reaction: Ms + Pl + Qz + Ilm + H2O = Kfs + Mag + Melt, followed by the fluid-absent melting reactions: Ms + Bt + Pl + Qz = Grt + Kfs + Melt. The protoliths underwent deep subduction and metamorphism during the Triassic, followed by exhumation to lower crust levels and subsequent remelting in the Late Jurassic. The fluid-present melting was likely triggered by aqueous fluid released from the underlying metamorphic rocks during the westward low-angle subduction of the Paleo-Pacific plate in the Jurassic. The resulting magmatism formed the Jingshan granites along the southeast margin of North China Block. These granites provide a compelling example of crustal recycling and rejuvenation during accretionary orogenesis, offering key insights into the evolution of continental crust during craton destruction. Published: 2026-05-08T11:47:59+00:00 Venue: Journal of Petrology Score: 0.583 (consider)

Journal: Journal of Petrology

DOI: 10.1093/petrology/egag043

Relevance: 0.583 (consider)

Abstract An integrated petrographic, mineralogic, geochronologic and phase equilibrium modelling study of Jingshan granites reveals four distinct metamorphic-anatectic-magmatic-hydrothermal events recorded in zircon and garnet. Zircon geochronology identifies Paleoproterozoic, Neoproterozoic and Triassic (240–200 Ma) ages in inherited domains, alongside Late Jurassic (~160 Ma) ages in anatectic/magmatic domains. These data indicate that the Jingshan granites originated from remelting of crustal materials that experienced Triassic metamorphism. Four generations of garnet are distinguished. Metamorphic garnet (Grt I), formed during Triassic metamorphism, exhibits flat to steep middle to heavy rare earth element (M-HREE) patterns. Peritectic garnet (Grt II), produced during Late Jurassic crustal anatexis, displays Y-HREE-enrichment and high (Yb/Dy)N ratios. Magmatic garnet (Grt III), crystallized during magma cooling, exhibits negative Eu anomalies and flat to steep M-HREE distributions. Hydrothermal garnet (Grt IV), formed during the late magmatic-hydrothermal stage, shows low TiO2 contents and positive Eu anomalies. Phase equilibrium modelling constrains partial melting conditions to 720–755°C and 1.2–1.3 GPa (corresponding to depths of ~ 38–44 km in the lower crust), occurring via the fluid-present melting reaction: Ms + Pl + Qz + Ilm + H2O = Kfs + Mag + Melt, followed by the fluid-absent melting reactions: Ms + Bt + Pl + Qz = Grt + Kfs + Melt. The protoliths underwent deep subduction and metamorphism during the Triassic, followed by exhumation to lower crust levels and subsequent remelting in the Late Jurassic. The fluid-present melting was likely triggered by aqueous fluid released from the underlying metamorphic rocks during the westward low-angle subduction of the Paleo-Pacific plate in the Jurassic. The resulting magmatism formed the Jingshan granites along the southeast margin of North China Block. These granites provide a compelling example of crustal recycling and rejuvenation during accretionary orogenesis, offering key insights into the evolution of continental crust during craton destruction.

item.title: Effect of hydrothermal alteration on the geochemical characteristics of mineralized porphyry: Example from the Xiaokelehe Cu Mo Deposit, NE China
item.dc:creator: Yunxuan Qin
Fanbo Meng
Xiaohu He
Jishuang Ding
Bizheng Yang
Weipeng Liu
Changzhou Deng
item.description: As globally significant sources of strategic metals, porphyry copper systems originate from magmatic-hydrothermal processes. In such systems, hydrothermal alteration typically overprints the ore-forming intrusions, obscuring their primary geochemical signatures and complicating petrogenetic interpretations based on whole-rock data. This study focuses on the granodiorite porphyry (148.2 ± 1.8 Ma) from the potassic and phyllic alteration zones of the Late Jurassic Xiaokelehe porphyry Cu Mo deposit in the Great Xing'an Range (GXR). By integrating geochronology, whole-rock geochemistry, and Isocon analysis with comparisons to fresh rocks, we evaluate elemental mass transfer during hydrothermal alteration and its implications for the reliability of conventional geochemical discrimination diagrams. Our results reveal a distinct spatial zonation in element mobility between the potassic and phyllic alteration zones. The potassic zone is characterized by significant enrichment of K, Si, and S, coupled with systematic depletion of Ca, Na, and Fe. In contrast, the phyllic alteration is characterized by the enrichment of Si and Fe along with intense leaching of Na, Mn, and Ca. Despite being overprinted by both alteration types, the granodiorite porphyries preserve typical adakitic signatures (e.g., high Sr/Y, low Yb, and right-sloping chondrite-normalized REE patterns), as demonstrated by comparative geochemistry. Together with low MgO and Ni, elevated K 2 O/Na 2 O, and high Sm/Yb ratios, these altered rocks exhibit a coherent geochemical fingerprint, indicating a shared magmatic source and petrogenetic evolution with the fresh rocks. Although alteration disturbs major elements and compromises conventional discrimination diagrams, this study demonstrates that a suite of trace elements and their ratios (e.g., REEs, Y, (La/Yb) N , Sm/Yb) exhibit strong resistance to alteration and can serve as reliable tracers for reconstructing magma-source characteristics and evolutionary histories. Based on these findings, we propose a systematic workflow that integrates alteration-type identification, Isocon-based quantitative analysis, and the prioritized application of alteration-resistant indicators. This approach establishes a methodological framework for conducting reliable petrogenetic interpretation using whole-rock geochemical data in strongly altered terrains, thereby extending the utility of whole-rock geochemistry in hydrothermal systems. Published: 2026-05-06T16:22:26+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geochemical Exploration Score: 0.582 (consider)

Although alteration disturbs major elements and compromises conventional discrimination diagrams, this study demonstrates that a suite of trace elements and their ratios (e.g., REEs, Y, (La/Yb) N , Sm/Yb) exhibit strong resistance to alteration and can serve as reliable tracers for reconstructing magma-source characteristics and evolutionary histories. item.title: Revisiting zircon-melt REE partitioning systematics: new insight into shallow crustal melting on early Earth
item.dc:creator: Sheng Shang
Haoxiong He
Wim van Westrenen
Fraukje M. Brouwer
Yanhao Lin
item.description: Trace element abundances in ancient magmatic zircon could provide unique insights into Hadean and early Archaean felsic magmatism, yet the rare earth element (REE) partition coefficients (D REE ) between zircon and melt needed to translate zircon abundances to melt abundances remain poorly constrained. We combined results of eight new high-temperature partitioning experiments (1000–1400 °C) with prior data to quantify D REE systematics and show their strong dependence on both temperature and melt composition. An empirical lattice strain partitioning model for trivalent REEs was established, enabling re-assessment of reconstructed parental melt compositions from the REE abundances in ancient zircons. The inferred chondrite-normalized (La/Yb) N ratios in zircons from the Hadean and early Archaean are lower than those of later Archaean TTGs and adakites. This suggests a transition from garnet-absent, shallow crustal melting in the earliest Earth to later Archaean magmatism dominated by the involvement of deeper garnet signatures Published: 2026-05-06T22:56:17+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.580 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Sheng Shang; Haoxiong He; Wim van Westrenen; Fraukje M. Brouwer; Yanhao Lin

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.04.039

Relevance: 0.580 (consider)

Trace element abundances in ancient magmatic zircon could provide unique insights into Hadean and early Archaean felsic magmatism, yet the rare earth element (REE) partition coefficients (D REE ) between zircon and melt needed to translate zircon abundances to melt abundances remain poorly constrained. We combined results of eight new high-temperature partitioning experiments (1000–1400 °C) with prior data to quantify D REE systematics and show their strong dependence on both temperature and melt composition. An empirical lattice strain partitioning model for trivalent REEs was established, enabling re-assessment of reconstructed parental melt compositions from the REE abundances in ancient zircons. The inferred chondrite-normalized (La/Yb) N ratios in zircons from the Hadean and early Archaean are lower than those of later Archaean TTGs and adakites. This suggests a transition from garnet-absent, shallow crustal melting in the earliest Earth to later Archaean magmatism dominated by the involvement of deeper garnet signatures

item.title: Experimentally determined nickel partitioning between mantle minerals and hydrous mafic melts at 1050–1250 °C: Evaluation of pyroxenite contribution to arc magma generation
item.dc:creator: Zhongxing Sun
Mengfei Ruan
Xiaolin Xiong
Yu Wang
Mingdi Gao
item.description: High-Ni olivine phenocrysts in arc magmas are commonly used to infer the contribution of pyroxenite components during sub-arc mantle melting. However, accurate mantle mineral-melt Ni partition coefficients ( D Ni ) under conditions relevant to sub-arc mantle melting and early differentiation of arc magmas remain poorly constrained, casting doubt on this interpretation. One principal challenge for determining reliable mineral-melt D Ni by experiments is mitigating the severe Ni loss to experimental capsules. Using the Ni-bearing capsule technique, we here determined D Ni between mantle minerals (olivine, orthopyroxene, clinopyroxene and spinel) and hydrous mafic melt (∼5–12 wt% MgO, ∼1–9 wt% H 2 O) at 1–1.5 GPa and 1050–1250 ℃, corresponding to conditions of arc magma generation and early differentiation. The experimental results show that D Ni values for olivine, orthopyroxene and clinopyroxene increase from 11.4 to 37.6, 3.5 to 11.5 and 3.5 to 6.4, respectively, with decreasing melt MgO content and temperature. These data, combined with screened literature data with limited Ni loss, are used to establish empirical expressions of mineral-melt D Ni as functions of melt MgO content and temperature. These expressions were applied to model Ni contents in melts derived from peridotite and pyroxenite sources, as well as Ni contents in olivine crystallized from these melts. The modeling results show that olivine crystallized from hydrous arc peridotite-derived melts exhibits lower Ni contents than the high-Ni olivine phenocrysts observed in some arc magmas. In contrast, olivine crystallized from pyroxenite-derived melts can reproduce the elevated Ni signatures in these olivine phenocrysts. Our results demonstrate that the pyroxenite contribution to generation of arc magmas might be more widespread than previous expected. Published: 2026-05-07T15:24:45+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.578 (consider)

High-Ni olivine phenocrysts in arc magmas are commonly used to infer the contribution of pyroxenite components during sub-arc mantle melting. The experimental results show that D Ni values for olivine, orthopyroxene and clinopyroxene increase from 11.4 to 37.6, 3.5 to 11.5 and 3.5 to 6.4, respectively, with decreasing melt MgO content and temperature. These expressions were applied to model Ni contents in melts derived from peridotite and pyroxenite sources, as well as Ni contents in olivine crystallized from these melts. The modeling results show that olivine crystallized from hydrous arc peridotite-derived melts exhibits lower Ni contents than the high-Ni olivine phenocrysts observed in some arc magmas. In contrast, olivine crystallized from pyroxenite-derived melts can reproduce the elevated Ni signatures in these olivine phenocrysts. Our results demonstrate that the pyroxenite contribution to generation of arc magmas might be more widespread than previous expected.

item.title: Synchronized Mascarene volcanism reveals 400 kyr cycles in melt supply from the Réunion plume
item.dc:creator: Vincent Famin
Xavier Quidelleur
Laurent Michon
Maëlis Arnould
item.description: Abstract Many hotspots display evidence of fluctuating magmatism through time, at periods of 1–20 Myr, reflecting variations in melt production and/or extraction within mantle plumes. Here we show that the Réunion hotspot exhibits ~10 times shorter fluctuations of magmatic activity. Using fieldwork, K-Ar geochronology, and geomorphology, we reconstruct the volcanic histories of Réunion and Mauritius, the youngest expressions of the Réunion hotspot. Our results reveal coeval phases of volcanic activity and repose on both islands over the past 3 Ma, with an average recurrence interval of 370 ± 202 kyr. Given the ~230 km separation between the islands and the heterogeneity of the underlying lithosphere, this large-scale volcanic synchronization is most consistent with quasi-periodic modulation of melt supply at mantle depths. The ~400 kyr timescale resembles the eruptive tempo of the Deccan Traps, suggesting that such short-period cyclicity may represent a long-lived characteristic of the Réunion hotspot. Published: 2026-05-07T00:34:55+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.577 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Vincent Famin; Xavier Quidelleur; Laurent Michon; Maëlis Arnould

Journal: Nature Communications

DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-72855-1

Relevance: 0.577 (consider)

Abstract Many hotspots display evidence of fluctuating magmatism through time, at periods of 1–20 Myr, reflecting variations in melt production and/or extraction within mantle plumes. Here we show that the Réunion hotspot exhibits ~10 times shorter fluctuations of magmatic activity. Using fieldwork, K-Ar geochronology, and geomorphology, we reconstruct the volcanic histories of Réunion and Mauritius, the youngest expressions of the Réunion hotspot. Our results reveal coeval phases of volcanic activity and repose on both islands over the past 3 Ma, with an average recurrence interval of 370 ± 202 kyr. Given the ~230 km separation between the islands and the heterogeneity of the underlying lithosphere, this large-scale volcanic synchronization is most consistent with quasi-periodic modulation of melt supply at mantle depths. The ~400 kyr timescale resembles the eruptive tempo of the Deccan Traps, suggesting that such short-period cyclicity may represent a long-lived characteristic of the Réunion hotspot.

item.title: Coeur d’Alene-type Ag-Zn-Pb vein mineralization in the Canadian Appalachians: geological, mineral chemistry, fluid inclusion and isotopic studies of the sail pond occurrence, Newfoundland, Canada
item.dc:creator: James Conliffe
Stephen J. Piercey
item.description: Structurally controlled Ag-Zn-Pb deposits are a distinct deposit class that has been suggested as a sub-class of orogenic gold deposits. The Sail Pond occurrence, Newfoundland, represents the first documented occurrence of this type in the Canadian Appalachians. We present the results of detailed geological, mineralogical and geochemical studies from Sail Pond, including mineral chemistry, fluid inclusion and sulphur, lead, carbon and oxygen isotope studies. Mineralization is in structurally controlled quartz- sphalerite-galena-tetrahedrite-tennantite veins in metamorphosed carbonate rocks, with up to 2% Ag in tetrahedrite and tennantite. Geological, isotopic and fluid inclusion studies show that mineralization occurred at high temperatures (> 300 °C) and at mesozonal crustal depths (> 6 km). Moderate salinity, aqueous-carbonic hydrothermal fluids were generated during the devolatilization of crustal sequences during prograde metamorphism, which leached metals from the nearby crustal rocks. Mineralization precipitated in open space fractures in brittle carbonate host rocks, where metalliferous fluids encountered a suitable sulfate source. Important similarities between typical orogenic gold deposit models and the mineralization at Sail Pond (and other structurally controlled Ag-Zn-Pb deposits in western North America) include the structural control on mineralization, association with regional scale greenschist facies metamorphism in accretionary orogenies and CO2-rich mineralizing fluids of probable metamorphic origin. This supports the classification of these deposits as Coeur d’Alene-type deposits, a subset of orogenic gold mineral deposits and highlights the exploration potential for this deposit type in the Appalachians and other metamorphosed sedimentary platforms globally. Published: 2026-05-08T02:01:21+00:00 Venue: Mineralium Deposita Score: 0.576 (consider)

Authors: James Conliffe; Stephen J. Piercey

Journal: Mineralium Deposita

DOI: 10.1007/s00126-026-01446-3

Relevance: 0.576 (consider)

Structurally controlled Ag-Zn-Pb deposits are a distinct deposit class that has been suggested as a sub-class of orogenic gold deposits. The Sail Pond occurrence, Newfoundland, represents the first documented occurrence of this type in the Canadian Appalachians. We present the results of detailed geological, mineralogical and geochemical studies from Sail Pond, including mineral chemistry, fluid inclusion and sulphur, lead, carbon and oxygen isotope studies. Mineralization is in structurally controlled quartz- sphalerite-galena-tetrahedrite-tennantite veins in metamorphosed carbonate rocks, with up to 2% Ag in tetrahedrite and tennantite. Geological, isotopic and fluid inclusion studies show that mineralization occurred at high temperatures (> 300 °C) and at mesozonal crustal depths (> 6 km). Moderate salinity, aqueous-carbonic hydrothermal fluids were generated during the devolatilization of crustal sequences during prograde metamorphism, which leached metals from the nearby crustal rocks. Mineralization precipitated in open space fractures in brittle carbonate host rocks, where metalliferous fluids encountered a suitable sulfate source. Important similarities between typical orogenic gold deposit models and the mineralization at Sail Pond (and other structurally controlled Ag-Zn-Pb deposits in western North America) include the structural control on mineralization, association with regional scale greenschist facies metamorphism in accretionary orogenies and CO2-rich mineralizing fluids of probable metamorphic origin. This supports the classification of these deposits as Coeur d’Alene-type deposits, a subset of orogenic gold mineral deposits and highlights the exploration potential for this deposit type in the Appalachians and other metamorphosed sedimentary platforms globally.

item.title: Garnet and epidote chemistry of the Dulong Sn-Zn-In polymetallic deposit, SW China: Constraints on the hydrothermal fluid evolution and implications for exploration
item.dc:creator: Qing Yang
Suo-Fei Xiong
Shao-Yong Jiang
Yi Zheng
Fan Xiao
Shu-Gang Xiao
item.description: Skarn deposits are major repositories for a range of critical metals, including Sn, Pb, Zn, Fe, W, Cu, and Au. The Dulong skarn-type Sn-Zn-In polymetallic deposit, located on the southern margin of the Youjiang Basin within the Laojunshan Sn-polymetallic ore area, records hydrothermal evolution linked to the enrichment of Sn and In. This study integrates garnet U Pb dating with systematic major and trace element analyses of garnet and epidote to elucidate the ore-formation processes. Three garnet generations (Grt-1, Grt-2, Grt-3) and one epidote generation (Ep) are recognized, with garnet compositions plotting within the andradite-grossular solid-solution series (And 9–71 , Gros 27–87 ). Grt-1 and Grt-3 are enriched in heavy rare earth elements (HREE) but depleted in light rare earth elements (LREE), with a prominent negative Eu anomaly. In contrast, Grt-2 shows LREE enrichment and HREE depletion, reflecting a high water-to-rock (W/R) ratio and higher oxygen fugacity ( f O 2 ) relative to Grt-1 and Grt-3. Grt-2 is further subdivided into two subgroups: Grt-2a, formed in a weakly acidic closed system, and Grt-2b in a weakly acidic to near-neutral open system. Variations in REEs and high-field-strength elements (HFSE: Nb, Ta, Zr, Hf) suggest that Grt-2 experienced more intense water-rock interaction compared to Grt-1 and Grt-3. Published: 2026-05-07T06:59:34+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geochemical Exploration Score: 0.574 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Qing Yang; Suo-Fei Xiong; Shao-Yong Jiang; Yi Zheng; Fan Xiao; Shu-Gang Xiao

Journal: Journal of Geochemical Exploration

DOI: 10.1016/j.gexplo.2026.108103

Relevance: 0.574 (consider)

Skarn deposits are major repositories for a range of critical metals, including Sn, Pb, Zn, Fe, W, Cu, and Au. The Dulong skarn-type Sn-Zn-In polymetallic deposit, located on the southern margin of the Youjiang Basin within the Laojunshan Sn-polymetallic ore area, records hydrothermal evolution linked to the enrichment of Sn and In. This study integrates garnet U Pb dating with systematic major and trace element analyses of garnet and epidote to elucidate the ore-formation processes. Three garnet generations (Grt-1, Grt-2, Grt-3) and one epidote generation (Ep) are recognized, with garnet compositions plotting within the andradite-grossular solid-solution series (And 9–71 , Gros 27–87 ). Grt-1 and Grt-3 are enriched in heavy rare earth elements (HREE) but depleted in light rare earth elements (LREE), with a prominent negative Eu anomaly. In contrast, Grt-2 shows LREE enrichment and HREE depletion, reflecting a high water-to-rock (W/R) ratio and higher oxygen fugacity ( f O 2 ) relative to Grt-1 and Grt-3. Grt-2 is further subdivided into two subgroups: Grt-2a, formed in a weakly acidic closed system, and Grt-2b in a weakly acidic to near-neutral open system. Variations in REEs and high-field-strength elements (HFSE: Nb, Ta, Zr, Hf) suggest that Grt-2 experienced more intense water-rock interaction compared to Grt-1 and Grt-3.

item.title: Seismic segmentation and slab dehydration beneath Java, Indonesia: Geodynamic and hazard implications
item.dc:creator: Fitriani Fitriani
Yingfeng Ji
Jun Yang
Haris Faheem
Waqar Ahmed
Ye Zhu
Shoichi Yoshioka
Nobuaki Suenaga
item.description: Java Island is the most populated volcanic arc in the world; however, along-strike seismic segmentation beneath the subducting slab remains incompletely understood due to limited observational constraints and the scarcity of high-resolution three-dimensional (3D) modeling. Focusing on the Java subduction zone, we investigate how slab dehydration-related fluid release correlates spatially with variations in seismicity along the plate-coupled interface. The results of advanced 3D thermal modeling reveal consistent spatial relationships among thermal structure, dehydration fronts, and earthquake distribution. We further identify notable seismic gaps and contrasting activity levels that may reflect variations in stress or interplate coupling. This study provides an improved framework for assessing seismic hazard assessment in fluid-rich subduction zones frequently struck by earthquakes. Published: 2026-05-09T15:17:06+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.573 (consider)

Authors: Fitriani Fitriani; Yingfeng Ji; Jun Yang; Haris Faheem; Waqar Ahmed; Ye Zhu; Shoichi Yoshioka; Nobuaki Suenaga

Journal: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences

DOI: 10.1016/j.jseaes.2026.107123

Relevance: 0.573 (consider)

Java Island is the most populated volcanic arc in the world; however, along-strike seismic segmentation beneath the subducting slab remains incompletely understood due to limited observational constraints and the scarcity of high-resolution three-dimensional (3D) modeling. Focusing on the Java subduction zone, we investigate how slab dehydration-related fluid release correlates spatially with variations in seismicity along the plate-coupled interface. The results of advanced 3D thermal modeling reveal consistent spatial relationships among thermal structure, dehydration fronts, and earthquake distribution. We further identify notable seismic gaps and contrasting activity levels that may reflect variations in stress or interplate coupling. This study provides an improved framework for assessing seismic hazard assessment in fluid-rich subduction zones frequently struck by earthquakes.

item.title: Petrogenesis and element enrichment of Miocene leucogranite in the Chongshan Shear Zone, SW China: Constraints from geochronology, geochemistry, and Hf-Sr-Nd isotopes
item.dc:creator: Liang Huang
Ye Zhou
Zai-Bo Sun
Feng Zhao
Yan Dao
Su-mei Tian
Shu-Cheng Tan
Xue-long Liu
Shi-tao Zhang
Jia-Xi Zhou
item.description: The Chongshan Shear Zone is a major boundary structure along the western margin of the Eurasian Plate and the Sanjiang region of western Yunnan province, SW China. It hosts abundant Miocene leucogranites, representing the southeastern extension of the Himalayan leucogranites. Based on difference in mineral grain sizes and mineral contents (biotite), the leucogranites are subdivided into types I and II . Both leucogranites exhibit high-silica, iron-depleted, potassium-rich, high-alkali, and peraluminous characteristics, belonging to the shoshonitic and high-K calc-alkaline series . Type I shows a tetrad effect in its rare-earth element (REE) pattern and has lower ΣREE contents, while type II displays a right-sloping REE pattern with moderate heavy REE fractionation. Furthermore, both leucogranites exhibit depletion in Ti, Ba, Hf, Sr and enrichment in Rb, Cs, Th, U, Ta . Zircon and monazite U-Pb geochronology studies indicate that the emplacement ages of both types of leucogranites range from 23.0 to 18.3 Ma , with both in-situ zircon ε Hf ( t ) and whole-rock ε Nd ( t ) values being negative, and the magma source is derived from the melting of Mesoproterozoic (1662–1153 Ma) continental crustal basement material. Both leucogranites are of partial melting origin : types I results from prolonged anatexis with slightly stronger fractional crystallization than types II , classified as muscovite fluid-fluxed melting, and types II forms via muscovite fluid-absent melting. Both types of leucogranites show significant enrichment in Bi and B mean enrichment coefficient of 23.7 and 28.6 relative to the upper crustal Clark value, respectively, and their enrichment primarily stems from the initially enriched magma source region, exhibiting favorable scientific research value and mineral exploration potential. During the late stage of the Himalayan orogeny , the lateral collision between the Indian and Eurasian plates caused large-scale partial melting in the Chongshan region, forming the two types of Miocene leucogranites studied here, and simultaneously triggered the development of the Chongshan Shear Zone. Published: 2026-05-06T15:25:49+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.572 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Liang Huang; Ye Zhou; Zai-Bo Sun; Feng Zhao; Yan Dao; Su-mei Tian; Shu-Cheng Tan; Xue-long Liu; Shi-tao Zhang; Jia-Xi Zhou

Journal: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences

DOI: 10.1016/j.jseaes.2026.107097

Relevance: 0.572 (consider)

The Chongshan Shear Zone is a major boundary structure along the western margin of the Eurasian Plate and the Sanjiang region of western Yunnan province, SW China. It hosts abundant Miocene leucogranites, representing the southeastern extension of the Himalayan leucogranites. Based on difference in mineral grain sizes and mineral contents (biotite), the leucogranites are subdivided into types I and II . Both leucogranites exhibit high-silica, iron-depleted, potassium-rich, high-alkali, and peraluminous characteristics, belonging to the shoshonitic and high-K calc-alkaline series . Type I shows a tetrad effect in its rare-earth element (REE) pattern and has lower ΣREE contents, while type II displays a right-sloping REE pattern with moderate heavy REE fractionation. Furthermore, both leucogranites exhibit depletion in Ti, Ba, Hf, Sr and enrichment in Rb, Cs, Th, U, Ta . Zircon and monazite U-Pb geochronology studies indicate that the emplacement ages of both types of leucogranites range from 23.0 to 18.3 Ma , with both in-situ zircon ε Hf ( t ) and whole-rock ε Nd ( t ) values being negative, and the magma source is derived from the melting of Mesoproterozoic (1662–1153 Ma) continental crustal basement material. Both leucogranites are of partial melting origin : types I results from prolonged anatexis with slightly stronger fractional crystallization than types II , classified as muscovite fluid-fluxed melting, and types II forms via muscovite fluid-absent melting. Both types of leucogranites show significant enrichment in Bi and B mean enrichment coefficient of 23.7 and 28.6 relative to the upper crustal Clark value, respectively, and their enrichment primarily stems from the initially enriched magma source region, exhibiting favorable scientific research value and mineral exploration potential. During the late stage of the Himalayan orogeny , the lateral collision between the Indian and Eurasian plates caused large-scale partial melting in the Chongshan region, forming the two types of Miocene leucogranites studied here, and simultaneously triggered the development of the Chongshan Shear Zone.

item.title: Tight regulation of Earth’s long-term temperature over Phanerozoic time
item.dc:creator: Dongyu Zheng
Alex G. Lipp
Alexander Farnsworth
Shufeng Li
Andrew S. Merdith
Khushboo Gurung
Mingcai Hou
Anqing Chen
Zixi Hou
Daniel J. Lunt
Erik A. Sperling
Paul J. Valdes
Benjamin J. W. Mills
item.description: Abstract Knowing the past temperature of the Earth is crucial for understanding the mechanisms driving climate change and biosphere evolution, but there is significant debate about the range of past temperature variation. Previous interpretations, largely based on oxygen isotope records, suggest that global temperature has generally declined over the past 539 million years, but substantial uncertainties persist. In this study, we introduce an independent estimate for long-term Phanerozoic temperature trends based on a large database of chemical weathering indices from siliciclastic sedimentary rocks, globally upscaled using a state-of-the-art general circulation paleoclimate model. Our results imply that Phanerozoic global temperatures remained within 10-30 °C, and that Paleozoic oceans had comparable temperatures to Mesozoic and Cenozoic oceans, in contrast to previous work suggesting that they were anomalously hot. This finding supports the idea that negative feedback processes, such as silicate weathering, have maintained long-term global average temperatures within a relatively tight range, contributing to the continued long-term evolution of the biosphere. Published: 2026-05-04T12:56:10+00:00 Venue: Nature Communications Score: 0.570 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Dongyu Zheng; Alex G. Lipp; Alexander Farnsworth; Shufeng Li; Andrew S. Merdith; Khushboo Gurung; Mingcai Hou; Anqing Chen; Zixi Hou; Daniel J. Lunt; Erik A. Sperling; Paul J. Valdes; Benjamin J. W. Mills

Journal: Nature Communications

DOI: 10.1038/s41467-026-72672-6

Relevance: 0.570 (consider)

Abstract Knowing the past temperature of the Earth is crucial for understanding the mechanisms driving climate change and biosphere evolution, but there is significant debate about the range of past temperature variation. Previous interpretations, largely based on oxygen isotope records, suggest that global temperature has generally declined over the past 539 million years, but substantial uncertainties persist. In this study, we introduce an independent estimate for long-term Phanerozoic temperature trends based on a large database of chemical weathering indices from siliciclastic sedimentary rocks, globally upscaled using a state-of-the-art general circulation paleoclimate model. Our results imply that Phanerozoic global temperatures remained within 10-30 °C, and that Paleozoic oceans had comparable temperatures to Mesozoic and Cenozoic oceans, in contrast to previous work suggesting that they were anomalously hot. This finding supports the idea that negative feedback processes, such as silicate weathering, have maintained long-term global average temperatures within a relatively tight range, contributing to the continued long-term evolution of the biosphere.
